data.title: Candel Therapeutics: 'Cancer Vaccine' Buy Thesis Appears Compelling
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Needham, Massachusetts-based biotech Candel Therapeutics, Inc. (CADL) was on the rise in trading yesterday, gaining ~13% for the day, rising to as value of ~$7.3 per share, market cap valuation of ~$530m.

The catalyst for gains appears to have been coverage from Cantor Fitzgerald, who gave Candel an overweight rating, and the stock a $30 price target. Analysts at Citi have also apparently set a price target of $26.

According to its 2025 annual report / 10-K filing, Candel is:

a clinical stage biopharmaceutical company focused on developing off-the-shelf viral immunotherapies that elicit an individualized, systemic anti-tumor immune response to help patients fight cancer.

Our engineered viruses are designed to induce a systemic anti-tumor response due to induction of immunogenic cell death within the tumor microenvironment, thus releasing tumor neo-antigens and creating a pro-inflammatory microenvironment at the site of injection.

This is intended to lead to in-situ immunization against the injected tumor and uninjected distant metastases. Local administration is designed to achieve these therapeutic effects while minimizing systemic exposure and associated toxicity.

An overview of Candel's business is also provided in a recent corporate presentation, as below:

As we can see, the company has two main pipeline candidates -aglatimagene besadenovec, or CAN-2409, which is in studies for prostate, pancreatic, and non-small cell lung cancer ("NSCLC"), and linoserpaturev, or CAN-3110, which is presently in an ongoing investigator-sponsored phase 1b clinical trial in the initial target indication of recurrent high-grade glioma ("HGG").

In its 2025 earnings press release, Paul Peter Tak, M.D., Ph.D., FMedSci, President and CEO of Candel is quoted as follows:

For aglatimagene in localized, intermediate-to high-risk prostate cancer, we continue to work toward a planned BLA submission in Q4 2026. We continue to follow the patients in our phase 3 study for prostate cancer-specific outcomes and expect to share results after extended follow-up in Q2 2026. We also expect to share additional biomarker data from our prostate program in Q3 2026. We plan to initiate a pivotal phase 3 clinical trial of aglatimagene in NSCLC despite ICI treatment in Q2 2026.

In summary, then, we may conclude that aglatimagene is the key value driver for Candel, being at a much later stage in the clinical development than linoserpaturev, and anticipate that there is a major near-term catalyst in play - Phase 3 prostate cancer data, which will be key to supporting the company's biologics license application ("BLA") requesting commercial approval for the drug in the U.S.

Analysis: Wall Street Sets High Price Targets As Lead Asset Looks Set For Approval

In my experience covering biotechs, it is usually wise to take analyst price targets with a pinch of salt - drug development is a challenging industry and there are no guarantees that a pipeline asset will secure approval, and become a commercial success. Still, with that said, the near-term catalyst in play for aglatimagene does feel likely to generate significant share price volatility.

When the company reported in December 2024 that aglatimagene had achieved its primary endpoint of a

"statistically significant and clinically meaningful benefit when combined with radiation therapy for intermediate-to-high risk, localized prostate cancer"

in a Phase 3 study "evaluating CAN-2409 viral immunotherapy in localized prostate cancer patients," its stock jumped in value from ~$5 per share, to >$9 per share.

Aglatimagene was administered in combo with a prodrug, valacyclovir (a treatment for genital herpes) - an explanation of the full mechanism of action ("MoA") is also included in the release, as follows:

CAN-2409 is an investigational, off-the-shelf, replication-defective adenovirus that delivers the herpes simplex virus thymidine kinase (HSV-tk) gene to tumor cells. CAN-2409, when administered with valacyclovir, is designed to induce immunogenic cell death of tumor cells with exposure of tumor antigens in the context of an activated tumor microenvironment.

Together, this regimen is designed to induce an individualized and specific CD8+ T cell-mediated response against the tumor, based on in situ vaccination against a variety of tumor antigens. Preclinical and clinical evidence suggests that CAN-2409 can be synergistic with local radiotherapy, providing further support for the design of the current phase 3 clinical trial.

Shortly after that, analysts at Bank of America suggested the drug could earn peak revenues of ~$2.9bn by 2029, which, if true, implies the current market cap of $530m undervalues the company by several billion dollars, I'd speculate.

By February last year, stock had climbed in value to >$12 per share, however a Phase 2 data release from a study of aglatimagene, plus standard of care chemoradiation, followed by resection, in patients with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma ("PDAC") triggered a sell-off to <$5 per share by mid-April, despite Candel reporting that:

the survival data showed notable improvement in estimated median overall survival of 31.4 months after treatment with CAN-2409 compared to only 12.5 months in the control group

In September, BofA analysts downgraded its price target to $7 per share, citing a lack of near-term catalysts, and in November, Candel announced it would development of aglatimagene in PDAC, to focus on prostate and NSCLC treatment instead.

Since then, until this week at least, Candel stock generally traded in a range of ~$4.5 - $6.5, but it does seem as though the market is now getting excited about the imminent prostate readout. In its latest 10-K filing, the company states:

  • We expect to announce supportive data on prostate cancer-specific outcomes (prostate cancer-specific DFS, time to salvage anti-cancer therapy, and time to metastasis) after extended follow-up in the second quarter of 2026.
  • In addition, in the third quarter of 2026, we expect to present novel immunological biomarker data in patients with localized prostate cancer.

Disease free survival is the primary endpoint, and the study has already shown it can "significantly" improve DFS and achieve a 30% risk reduction in disease recurrence vs the placebo arm, and a 38% reduction in prostate cancer specific disease recurrence, while 2-year biopsies showed a complete response ("CR") response in 80.4% of patients, versus 63.6% in the placebo arm. Finally, Candel has noted a:

compelling safety profile, with lower incidence of serious adverse events (SAEs) and treatment-related SAEs in active arm vs control (5.8% vs 7.3% and 1.7% vs 2.2%, respectively)

As such, the upcoming data readout seems more likely than not to confirm a positive treatment effect in patients, allowing Candel to file its BLA and, as we can see below, the company has already been planning for a commercial launch:

Candel also shares an overview of the costs of current prostate cancer treatments, as shown below:

As we can see, there are several different types of therapy already available to treat the disease - the likes of Pfizer's (PFE) Xtandi and Novartis' (NVS) Pluvicto earned ~$2bn of revenues in 2025 - but because aglatimagene is an adjunctive therapy, it may command a lower price point than rivals, and insurers may be reluctant to reimburse for its use on top of current SoCs, although as the company points out, if the drug is included in NCCN guidelines, insurers may have no choice but to cover regardless of price.

Analysis: What's The Play On Candel Today?

We should not forget that as well as prostate, aglatimagene has shared some strong data in NSCLC too - as per Candel's 10-K:

In patients with an inadequate response to immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) treatment who received 2 aglatimagene plus valacyclovir courses (Cohort 1+2, per protocol population, n=46), mOS (median overall survival) was 24.5 months.

In patients with progressive disease, despite ICI treatment (Cohort 2, per protocol population, n=41), mOS was 21.5 months, which is markedly longer than the 9.8–11.8 months of survival reported in published literature in a similar patient population receiving standard of care of docetaxel second-line chemotherapy (Paz-Ares LG et al, J Clin Oncol 2024;42:2860-2872 ; Ahn MJ et al, J Clin Onc 2024;43:260-272).

With lung cancer being the largest oncology market, Candel has a second opportunity to potentially drive billions of dollars per annum in revenues, however, given the difference between analysts price targets and peak revenue speculation, and Candel's current <$550m market cap, it's important to discuss risks to the bull thesis.

As of the end of 2025, Candel reported:

Cash and cash equivalents of $119.7 million, as of December 31, 2025, together with $93.5 million in net proceeds from the February 2026 follow-on equity offering, strengthens the Company’s financial position and is sufficient to fund the current operating plan into Q1 2028, which includes activities to support the potential commercial launch of aglatimagene in 2027

Candel made a net loss of $(38.2m), or $(0.72) per share in 2025, and while costs are bound to ramp up if the company secures commercial approval and bankrolls a full launch, current financing does seem sufficient, as management suggests.

My main concern would therefore turn to whether data is strong enough to persuade physicians and patients to prescribe and receive the drug on top of what would likely be an onerous regime of other drugs?

For example, Pharmas Merck (MRK) and Moderna (MRNA) have developed a similar "cancer vaccine" style product in Intismeran Autogene, which studies have shown reduced the risk of recurrence or death by 49% compared to KEYTRUDA alone in patients with melanoma, yet the drug seems no closer to securing a commercial approval today than it did when positive data was first reported some years ago.

That makes me wonder if Candel will find it harder than expected to convince the FDA to approve its drug, although I have to admit, the path ahead for aglatimagene does seem very clear, and approval very much achievable. A final question is whether Candel can make aglatimagene profitable?

Could it be the case that the company may spend hundreds of millions creating awareness of its product and offering substantial rebates and price cuts, only to discover physicians and patients are showing a preference for alternative treatments? That has certainly happened with other biotechs I have covered in the past. A final question might be why, with aglatimagene producing some strong results, no Pharmas have expressed interest in partnering with the company?

All things considered, however, these criticisms of the bull case do feel quite hypothetical, while the data the drug has produced to date and the discussions already had with the FDA seem to make a strong case that aglatimagene will be approved, towards the end of 2027.

Back in 2023, Candel stock slipped <$0.6 per share, before being rescued by its positive pancreatic data for aglatimagene. The company has therefore staged a remarkable comeback, even if its pancreatic studies have been put on hold.

Ultimately, while highlighting the fact that any pre-commercial biotech investment opportunity is inherently risky, and my suspicion that the pharmaceutical community does not completely buy into the "cancer vaccine" approach - I assign little value to the second pipeline asset, an oncololytic therapy, given the recent woes experienced by Replimmune (REPL) - I can't point to a significant enough flaw in the Buy thesis to undermine it, so find myself in agreement with Wall Street analysts that this is a stock with a good chance of doubling or even tripling in value, because its lead product appears to enhance current treatment paradigms in a hard-to-treat disease indication, and potentially, two, if we include NSCLC, both of which are multi-billion dollar treatment markets.

data.title: SpaceX: $1.75T IPO And 220x EV/EBITDA Could Trigger Surge In Aerospace And Defense
data.description:
SpaceX (SPACE) is set for a blockbuster IPO later this year that could value the company north of $1.75 trillion. In this report, I discuss why this is a mind-blowing valuation in a negative sense and why it may also result in the aerospace and defense sector, and particularly space stocks, substantially increasing in value.

SpaceX Is Not Pure Space

SpaceX consists of the launch services, and I would say the company has taken the right approach there by not filing for an IPO when it was still maturing the business and instead considering an IPO once it had a strong launch cadence. The other part of the company and likely the profit driver is Starlink, which provides satellite-internet solutions. Both of these elements are pure space. What makes SpaceX less pure-space is that it acquired xAI. That does set the company up to become the first vertically integrated space-AI company. However, I ran a preliminary sizing assessment on space-based GPUs and found that currently, the economics do not add up, and so space-based AI would only work if there is really no space (no pun intended) for AI data centers on Earth.

$1.75 Trillion Values The Company Through 2030

A $1.75 trillion valuation would be huge. According to Reuters, the company generated $8 billion in EBITDA in 2025 on $15-$16 billion in sales. So, that is around 50% in EBITDA margins. If we simply divide $1.75 trillion by $8 billion, we get an EV/EBITDA of roughly 220x. As a reference, the aerospace and defense industry is currently trading at around 24x EV/EBITDA. That would imply substantial overvaluation against current-year expected earnings.

However, even SpaceX is likely a name that one would buy at elevated prices for its value generation in the years ahead and likely into the next decade.

If we apply a growth assumption of 30% and each 10% growth adds one point in EBITDA margins, we note that the stock would be trading at 45.7x EV/EBITDA by 2030. Typically, I value space stocks at around 2x the aerospace and defense multiple. So, a $1.75 trillion valuation would value SpaceX through 2030, which I believe is not unreasonable. So, $1.75 trillion seems high but is not completely unrealistic. On EV/Sales, a $1.75 trillion valuation would imply 30.6x EV/Sales in the best case.

SpaceX IPO Could Lift The Entire Sector

The IPO could lift the aerospace and defense valuation multiples substantially. The image above provides a screenshot of the enterprise value monitor developed by The Aerospace Forum. What we see is that currently, the entire aerospace and defense industry is valued at $3.2 trillion. A $1.75 trillion SpaceX IPO would add nearly 55% to that, and that is the kind of addition that would likely also drive a substantial rerating for the aerospace and defense industry as a whole, with strong rerating potential for more space-focused companies.

We also see that somewhat back in the EV/EBITDA and EV/Sales that a $1.75 trillion valuation would suggest. For space companies, I generally believe that 2x-3x a peer group valuation can be justified for EV/EBITDA. SpaceX would have an average multiple of 93.6x through 2030, which would indicate nearly four times the peer group. On EV/Sales, I typically use the multiple of Rocket Lab as the standard for emerging aerospace and defense companies, which is around four times the peer group multiple of 4.3x. The average of SpaceX would be 55.4x between 2026 and 2030, or nearly 13 times the peer group multiple. So, with the weight of SpaceX against peers and its valuation multiples, it almost cannot be any different from a re-rating being required for the entire industry.

Some pure-play space companies are Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB), Intuitive Machines, Inc. (LUNR), Redwire Corporation (RDW), Planet Labs PBC (PL), and Satellogic Inc. (SATL), with many companies having space and missile exposure, and in the supply chain, there is a mix of commercial, defense, and space exposure as well. So, if this IPO is as big as we see today, then a major reset on valuations across the aerospace and defense, and particularly in the space domain, is required. Given how elaborate supply chains are, it should drive a substantial increase in multiples ranging from pure-play space to defense primes and into the supply chain.

Conclusion: SpaceX Could Be A Buy And Lift The Aerospace And Defense Industry With It

A $1.75 trillion valuation would be insanely high, but at the same time, if the company can grow at a rate of 30% annually, it would also make some sense. It would also imply that current scaling companies and mature companies are substantially undervalued, and as a result, I do believe that a $1.75 trillion SpaceX IPO would be bullish not only for SpaceX but for the industry as a whole.

data.title: Agnico Eagle's Project Pipeline Will Propel Growth, And Not Just Bullion
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) has current cash flow strength, low geopolitical risk and visible internal growth pipeline. And this is why I am rating the company a Strong Buy. I mean the company sits in a sweet spot that most gold miners never quite reach. As I said, it generates great FCFs at scale, operates in lower risk jurisdiction and has a genuine internal pipeline. I believe this pipeline can add value well beyond what spot gold alone explains. I am saying this because in 4Q 2025 AEM produced 3.45 Moz, delivered record operating cash flows of $6.82Bn and FCFs of $4.4Bn. I also note that the company's management raised the dividend by 12.5% and ended the year at a net cash position of $2.67Bn.

The theme that I want to focus on is that AEM is evolving from a high quality gold producer into a long duration Canadian growth platform. And this is important because the market often values miners on near term ounces and spot commodity moves, while underpricing de-risked future production. The company believes that it can lift annual production by 20% to 30% over the next 10 years with the potential to exceed 4 Moz in the early 2030s. I believe that is quite a big statement. Though, more importantly it is increasingly backed by real Canadian assets. I do think that Canadian Malartic, Detour Lake, Upper Beaver and Hope Bay are moving through real developments milestone. And I believe that gives my thesis more weight. The CEO, Ammar Al-Joundi put it correctly when he said that AEM is in the "strongest financial position in our company's history, and we believe we are exceptionally well positioned in the current gold price environment." He also mentioned that the company's project pipeline can drive strong production growth over the next decade. I believe this quote captures why AEM is positioned for the future and not based on the near-term oz that it produces. It is not trying to win the chessboard with one flashy move. It is also building a position after position until the endgame looks unfair.

Business Overview

Agnico Eagle is one of the world's largest gold miners. It has its operations concentrated in Canada, Australia, Finland and Mexico. The company is intentional about having its portfolio in countries with low political risk. I am saying this because I recently analyzed Newmont (NEM) and one of the key risks was its mines in Ghana. You can refer to the article here. Also, I note that Barrick Mining (B) has had disputes in Mali. You can read more on B here. The reason I brought this up is because geography can wreck a good model overnight.

And what I note is that investors may still underappreciate that AEM is not just a collection of mature mines. It is also increasingly a network of regional hubs with expansion potential. For instance, Detour Lake already has the largest gold minerals in Canada and a life cycle into the 2050s. Canadian Malartic is transitioning underground through Odyssey while the company studies a path towards 1 Moz of annual production beginning in 2033. With that kind of embedded optionality, I believe that AEM deserves a better growth multiple than a plain vanilla gold producer.

Growth Drivers

First of all, as I said earlier, the company is already strong enough to support my investment thesis. AEM's 2025 gold production stood at 3.45 Moz. This is above the midpoint of their guidance. On the other side, FCFs reached a record number of $4.4Bn. This means that even with higher royalty costs because realized gold process were strong, the company still delivered 2025 AISC of $ 1313/oz. For 2026, AISC guidance stands at $1475/oz which is still higher. But it also leaves a huge margin cushion in a gold market that remains fundamentally supportive. According to the outlook from World Gold Council, they expect 2026 to benefit from elevated central bank demand, strong ETF inflows and geopolitical support for investment demand. The World Bank also mentioned that gold prices are expected to remain elevated into 2026. And this is supported by safe haven demand and central bank buying.

The second growth driver that I see is Canadian Malartic. I am saying this because once Barnat is depleted in 2029, annual production is expected to be between 550,000 and 600,000 oz from underground source. And there is also about 40k tpd of excess mill capacity. As of now, AEM is studying a second shaft plus Marban and Wasamac to potentially ramp the complex towards 1 Moz annually beginning in 2033. In my view that is not just a minor optimization. To me if the execution keeps improving, then I believe it is a full rerating candidate. Also, East Gouldie production from the ramp is expected in Q1 2026 with shaft hoisted production expected in Q2 2027.

Then there is Detour Lake which I highlighted previously. It produced 692 675 oz in 2025. And in 2026 it has a midpoint guidance of 715 000 oz. It is important to highlight that AEM is advancing a path to potentially take Detour toward 1 Moz of annual production as underground work progress. Why am I saying this? Because Detour is already a scale asset. And when a giant asset gets bigger, then the market tends to notice a little late.

Fourth is Upper Beaver. And this is where I think hidden value starts to become tangible. Beaver has the potential to produce 200 000 to 225 000 oz of gold annually plus 3 600 tonnes of copper. The project is being advanced through a phased approach with an exploration ramp and shaft to de-risk the development. My rough valuation using the midpoint of 212k oz, a conservative $1500/oz and a 5X operating cashflow multiple gets me to about $1.6Bn of NAV before even the copper upside. Using $501 Mn shares outstanding, that alone implies about $3 to $4 per share of incremental value.

Lastly there is Hope Bay which is still earlier. But I would say it is bigger than many appreciate. The project could support between 400 000 and 425 000 oz of annual gold production at 6000 tpd. And it is considering a construction decision in H1 2026. I am not going to fully capitalize Hope Bay yet because that would be getting ahead of the shovel. But I do think the market is assigning too little option value here. When I put Upper Beaver together with some profitability adjusted value for Hope Bay and the Canadian Malartic expansion path, then I think AEM embedded project pipeline could reasonably be worth $8 to $12 per share beyond what static multiples capture today.

With earnings around the corner, I the next earnings report matters less for the headline EPS beat and more about what management says about the moving parts inside my thesis. First, I will be watching whether production stays on track across detour Lake, Canadian Malartic and Nunavut operations. And this is because that is where confidence in the multiyear volume outlook is either reinforced or chipped away. Then secondly, I will be on the lookout for a clear commentary on cost discipline, especially around site level operating costs, sustaining capital and any pressure on 2026 AISC guidance. In additional, I will be also keen on commentary around timing and derisking of Odyssey, Upper Beaver and Hope Bay. And this is because I believe those are projects that can add real incremental value beyond what the market is currently pricing.

Valuation

When I look at the valuation grades below, I note a couple of things. First is that on TTM numbers, AEM looks rich and is trading at a premium relative to the sector median. For instance the TTM Non-GAAP P/E stands at 26.42x while the sector median is at 19.36x. Again, TTM EV/sales shows that AEM trades at 9.05x while the sector median trades at 2.12x. This is a premium of 312%.

The more useful numbers in the screenshot above are the Forward [FWD]. AEM's FWD non-GAAP P/E is at 16.38x which is basically almost in line with the sector median at 16.44x. FWD EV/EBITDA is at 8.86x which is slightly above the sector median of 8.58x. But the standout is the FWD non-GAAP PEG which stands at 0.8x. And it is 39.11% below the 1.32x sector median. That combination tells me that the discount is far less dramatic when I account for earnings growth and on a growth adjusted basis. From that angle, AEM looks attractive. And that is just what I need. While AEM may look expensive on other multiples like TTM, it is not expensive relative to the growth engine underneath it.

Compared to other peers like NEM, B and Kinross (KGC) I believe AEM deserves to sit toward the premium end because its asset base is more concentrated in lower risk jurisdictions as I earlier mentioned. Also, its management is funding growth from a position of net cash rather than balance sheet strain. Fitch Ratings previously highlighted AEM's operations in favorable jurisdictions and expected leverage discipline. And this still lines up with what I am seeing now. So yes, AEM may not be the cheapest stock in the gold batch. Because I actually think it doesn't need to be. I believe in mining, paying a fair price for quality often beats buying cheap in a bad postcode.

Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for AEM is project execution risk. I have outlined a couple of project pipeline that AEM had. And if Canadian Malartic, Upper Beaver or Hope Bay slip meaningfully on timing or capital intensity, then it means that some of the growth value I am underwriting will move to the right. And the market does not like waiting. That scenario would likely compress the premium multiple.

Final Thoughts

I like Agnico Eagle because it gives me exposure to gold without forcing me to accept a messy and risky jurisdiction profile. Or a stretched balance sheet. Or a one asset hope kind of a trade. The current business is already delivering. The balance sheet is already strong. And the project pipeline is getting real enough to think the market will increasingly have to price it in. This is why I am rating AEM a Strong Buy.

data.title: NuScale: Nuclear Hype, But Limited Near-Term Revenue
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NuScale Power Corporation's (SMR) valuation doesn't appear to reflect its actual business realities, since the company is unlikely to directly benefit from rising nuclear demand. General nuclear growth is driven by policy, which supports all players within the nuclear value chain. However, this has a more immediate impact on miners and enrichers, whereas NuScale is a more upstream player with a longer timeline to benefit. Therefore, the company will need to secure a fully funded, legally binding construction agreement before they can start generating sustainable revenue. It appears the market has assumed broader nuclear industry trends would benefit NuScale, which gave the stock a premium without any real movement towards deploying reactors or generating scalable revenue.

In addition to this disconnect are the execution risks associated with ENTRA1 Energy, the exclusive commercialization partner for NuScale. To add to this, there is little evidence in NuScale's financials that indicates progress, with declining revenue, operating losses, and constant dilution. Even NuScale's most advanced project in Romania will take years to generate meaningful returns, and for the next several years, the primary source of revenue will likely be low-margin license fees and engineering services. With this large disconnect between valuation and fundamentals, I'm rating the stock a Sell.

Nuclear Demand Doesn't Equate to NuScale Revenue

It was recently reported that the US is looking to increase uranium imports from Namibia (the world’s third largest uranium producing country), and is also exploring options to increase production domestically. This news is positive for the overall nuclear industry, with AI-driven power demand expectations beginning to turn into reality. This is still a long way away from benefiting an upstream technology licensor like NuScale, which has no immediate role in mining or enriching.

The company is developing and marketing small modular reactors (SMRs), designed to be licensed to project developers. Therefore, none of NuScale’s business operations or activities occur within the uranium supply chain. The market reacted positively to this recent Namibia news, and the stock rose over 10% on the day of the announcement. However, Namibia exporting more uranium to the US doesn't help move up NuScale's timeline for the construction on the first project. Overall, the company appears years away from scalable revenue, and much of the benefit seems priced in, with the stock trading at ~137x trailing revenue.

ENTRA1 Risks

The core of NuScale’s U.S. commercialization plan has always been through ENTRA1, the company's worldwide exclusive global strategic partner. This is under their Partnership Milestone Agreement (PMA), which recently triggered a payment of ~$507.4 million to its partner for Milestone Contribution 1 under the PMA's terms. This agreement was made with the intent that ENTRA1 would deploy NuScale's technology commercially, with a headline non-binding deal with the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) to deploy up to 6 GW of SMR capacity. For each NuScale Power Module (NPM) deployed, ENTRA1 is entitled to compensation of $35-55 million. With a potential 72 NPMs set to be deployed, ENTRA1's payout at the midpoint would be ~$3.25 billion, which could cause significant dilution.

Financials

NuScale earned $31.5 million in total revenue during FY 2025, a 15% YoY decrease. A significant portion of that revenue was generated from the RoPower Technology License Agreement and Fluor FEED Engineering Services, with no reactors purchased or constructed. Net loss totaled $664.5 Million, largely led by an ENTRA1 milestone payment of $507.4 million.

The only way NuScale can continue to operate is through dilution via the sale of stock. In Q4 2025 alone, the company issued 39.3 million shares through its ATM program and raised $750 million in gross proceeds. As a result of this activity throughout the year, NuScale had $1.3 billion in cash and investments at the end of FY2025. Management expects operating expense to stay between $170-200 million, giving the company 5-6 years of runway.

This appears like a long runway, but could easily be wiped away by potential ENTRA1 milestone payouts.

Due to this potential and the long timeline until commercialization, I believe NuScale will eventually need to raise more capital.

Romania Progress

The company's nearest-term chance to see their reactor deployed is with the Romanian RoPower Doicești project. Nuclearelectrica made a Final Investment Decision (FID) in February 2026, as NuScale finished its FEED Phase 2 Study. The planned power will be comprised of 462 MW of combined electricity produced by six 77 MW NuScale reactors.

Even with the progress, the timeline is still extensive. After the FID was announced, the project is entering a 15-month Pre EPC phase to include completing a structure for a pre-EPC contract, finding contractors, extension of technology license agreements, and environmental impact assessment agreements. Given this and other phases, first commercial operation is set for 2033. Any money NuScale makes from Romania prior to commissioning will have been for licensing and engineering services, which means low-margin, project-paced income which will have little impact on the income statement. The initial investment for this site is expected to be around $6-7 billion, much of which will not go to NuScale. Despite this and the long timeline until operation, the stock trades at a valuation ~$4.3 billion, which is over half the value of the initial investment for Romania's power plant as a whole. With this being the company's nearest term project, the valuation appears disconnected from fundamentals.

Broad Nuclear vs. NuScale

With AI-driven clean energy demand, nuclear investments have seen an uptick in valuation. For some nuclear-related companies, benefits are likely to come soon with developments such as Namibia export increases. This includes companies like Cameco (CCJ) and Centrus Energy (LEU) who sit directly in that supply chain and benefit directly from uranium demand.

Companies like NuScale don't fit as well in this overall nuclear supply chain. While they could eventually benefit, they are ultimately technology licensors who have designed a reactor and are attempting to attract credit-worthy customers. Those interested would have to then finance, design, build and operate the reactors with each site being funded at a cost of $2-10 billion. Financing and executing on these large-scale projects is much harder than engineering them. This is common in large-scale projects like these, with NuScale's previous flagship project falling through after the initial investment cost tripled. With the Romania project being non-binding, the potential of the project falling through is certainly there. This combined with potential far out revenues makes it hard to justify the company's current valuation.

Valuation

At a current valuation of $4.3 billion, SMR trades ~137x trailing revenue of $31.5 million, which has declined over the past year. The company currently has no earnings, with no timeline to profitability and first expected deployments in 2033. Over the next few years NuScale may reasonably earn revenue from Romania licensing fees and small DOE development. Those represent low-margin revenue streams, making the ~$1.3 billion cash balance a key component of the stock's valuation. Currently, this cash value adds up to ~$4.01 per share when divided by shares outstanding of ~324 million. This leaves over $8 per share in premium for nuclear operations. Given the non-binding, long-term agreement that the company currently has, this premium appears overstated. This is especially true given the risks that come with construction of these plants, which can add heavy costs and delays into the mix. In my view, this $8 per share premium appears too high given the company's place in the supply chain for nuclear demand, which is why I'm rating the stock a Sell.

Risks

If ENTRA1 signs a new binding partnership with an organization like the TVA and is able to quickly start up construction, it could cause NuScale's valuation to increase. This would show near-term development and could shorten timeline to scalable revenue dramatically.

Any updates in policy could cause NuScale's valuation to surge due to hype. This has begun to happen throughout the few years due to expected nuclear demand and could occur again if developments, like the Namibia exports, continue to release.

Conclusion

NuScale appears to represent a company trending up based on a long-term theme. However, this theme appears too broad for a company like NuScale with first commercial deployments expected by 2033. These construction projects also carry considerable risk, with project prices often increasing over time. This is evidenced by their previous Idaho project, and makes the current project considerably riskier, especially when considering the non-binding agreement. NuScale also makes considerable payouts to ENTRA1, which could add up over time with more milestones and deployments. With 5-6 years of runway at current liquidity, any more of those payouts could shorten it.

Unless NuScale manages to secure a binding, fully-funded contract, the stock could potentially return closer to the value of its liquidity. While there is potential upside with long-term nuclear developments, the company appears to be too far down the supply chain and too far away from commercial revenues. Due to this, I'm rating the stock a Sell.

data.title: Marvell: The Market Still Underestimates The Growth Potential
data.description:
The Marvell Investment Thesis

In my opinion, Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) continues to be underestimated. Although the multiple expansion potential has been exhausted, there is still margin expansion potential and significant upside in terms of revenue growth. Marvell has positioned itself extremely well in recent months, and it appears that the market is still somewhat negative when we look at the consensus estimates.

I still consider these estimates to be too low, which is why I am maintaining my Buy rating, even after the strong rally of the last few weeks.

What was my previous coverage like?

I first wrote about Marvell for the Best GARP Idea Contest on Seeking Alpha. And my idea has worked out quite well because the shares have increased by ~120% since then. At the time, my view was that Marvell had multiple upside potential, as they were cheaper than the competition. I also saw significant growth potential in terms of revenue, thanks to Marvell's connectivity solutions and support in the area of effective memory usage. Moreover, I also saw potential for margin expansion, especially through the divestment of the automotive Ethernet businesses.

Since my first article, I have written several more, each focusing on different areas. In January of this year, I took a closer look at the XConn acquisition and why it could be a game-changer. At the beginning of March, I took a closer look at the FY26 results and wrote something about the ongoing rumors about the XPU losses, which had a negative impact on sentiment towards Marvell.

Now, I want to see how the latest news can be interpreted and what caused Marvell's strong increase in the share price.

MRVL's increase is driven by multiple expansion

When I wrote my first article, Marvell's forward P/E ratio was 24x. This was much too low for a company growing by more than 40%. The current forward multiple of 38x is fair, in my opinion. This multiple fairly reflects Marvell's competitive position and growth potential. But as a result, I don't see any further multiple upside potential. This means that share price growth must primarily be driven by earnings growth.

How have Marvel's margins changed?

When I wrote my first article, Marvell's EBIT margin was 10%. Since then, it has risen to 16%. But I still see potential for further margin expansion.

The competition still has better EBIT margins, and I see potential to reach an EBIT margin of over 20% by the end of the year. However, I consider it rather unrealistic to achieve a margin similar to that of Broadcom Inc. (AVGO). Broadcom is simply too big right now and has a market position with significantly more pricing power.

What could drive earnings growth?

Firstly, of course, the demand for AI is still higher than the supply, and therefore, we have not yet reached peak earnings. Most analysts have also underestimated how strong the growth has been in recent years, and I think this will continue over the next two years. I think most estimates are still too conservative.

The revenue estimate for FY27 is currently at $10.86 billion, but I think it will be closer to $11 billion–$11.3 billion. And I also think that the revenue growth of 28% in the fiscal period ending in January 2029 is too low. If Marvell actually collaborates with Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) to develop two new chips, it would be very beneficial for the revenues. The testing phase would probably take place in 2026 and 2027. But from 2028 onwards, these two chips could lead to a significant increase in revenue. Although Alphabet will probably have pricing power in this project, and this will slightly reduce Marvell's margin, it should still lead to growth in revenue and profits.

I also see the strategic partnership with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) as a positive sign for the future. In my opinion, Nvidia seems to have recognized that Marvell is a strong player in the connectivity sector, which is extremely important for expanding AI data centers.

Barclays, for example, predicts that Marvell's optics division could grow by 90% this year and that earnings could reach $5.

Valuation

Barclays' $5 EPS target lies above the consensus, which currently assumes that the EPS for FY27 will be $3.83. Personally, I also tend to the $5 mark and consider it realistic.

Earlier, we looked at the forward P/E ratio, which stood at 38x. But we use the TTM P/E ratio for the price target. This TTM P/E is now at 47x, which is, of course, already relatively expensive. So, I think it's better to expect a multiple of 40x at the end of the fiscal year to have a safety margin.

  • Exit Multiple 40 x $5 = $200.

So, my price target for FY27 is $200, which represents an upside of ~35% from the current share price of ~$150.

Risks to my thesis

TrendForce published a list this month in which Marvell is listed as a design provider for Trainium 2 and Maia 300. This suggests that Alchip has apparently won the Trainium 3 and Trainium 4 competition. Additionally, it can be seen here that Broadcom and MediaTek Inc. (MDTK.F) are already working with Alphabet on TPUs. So it could be difficult to achieve real design wins here.

Conclusion

I think that Marvel's focus on connectivity will bear fruit, and the company will definitely grow rapidly over the next three years. Design wins, such as with Alphabet, would then be the icing on the cake. But even if these do not come to fruition, the company is well-positioned. This is why I continue to see Marvell as a Buy, with a price target of $200 for the 2027 financial year.

The execution of the last few months has been very strong, and Marvell has shown that the company is one of the world's leading companies in its niche and is future-oriented. I believe that the XConn and Celestial acquisitions will strengthen the company's position within a year. In general, Marvell could benefit from the fact that its solutions could help to overcome the memory bottleneck.

data.title: OneMain Holdings: Dividend Yields And Liquidity Are Main Reasons For Adding It
data.description:
Three months had already passed since my previous coverage of OneMain Holdings, Inc. (OMF). And despite its promising fundamentals and dividends, the stock price dropped by 13%. Yet, I understand the cautious behavior of many investors towards stocks in the financial sector in line with increased macroeconomic volatility. On a lighter note, the recent price correction to $50 has opened new entry opportunities. Valuation has become more attractive and realistic. Technicals also show rebound attempts but still warrant some caution due to potential overbuying.

OneMain Holdings Has Remained Solid Lately

Macroeconomic volatility has remained evident in the past six months despite the efforts of the Fed to keep inflation under control. Tariff wars and the geopolitical tension in the Middle East fueled the situation. As a bank-like company, OneMain Holdings, Inc. is susceptible to volatility risks due to its cyclicality and sensitivity to indicators like inflation and interest rates. Even so, its prudent and disciplined loan management and asset diversification allow it to protect and even expand its operating capacity. We have seen this in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its interest income amounted to $1.42B, up by 7.2% YoY from $1.32B and 1.7% QoQ from $1.39B. This was impressive considering the policy easing cycle by the Fed in the second half of 2025. This should not be surprising because a substantial portion of its loans have fixed rates. For instance, its personal loans and auto loans are non-revolving loans with fixed rates. More importantly, its loan volume kept increasing because lower interest rates meant lower borrowing costs, which attracted new loans. For instance, the quantity of its personal loans reached 2.4M, which was equivalent to $21.4B. If you look at its balance sheet, its loan volume has increased in the past five years.

Likewise, its interest expense increased because its debt increased as well. But because of its strategic loan yields, the increase was easily offset. As a result, its net interest income kept increasing. Even if it increased its provisions to be more conservative, the YoY amount rose. Additionally, its other revenues rose YoY and QoQ. After all, more loans and investments mean more commissions, fees, and trading gains. As a result, its net income margin reached 27.3% versus 26.7% QoQ and 19.5% YoY.

Areas of Consideration

As we all know, OMF is susceptible to inflationary headwinds aggravated by tariff woes. This should not be surprising since its loans are basically loans to individuals and households. This makes it exposed to risks associated with higher prices and volatile interest rates because these types of loans are typically unsecured. Currently, inflation has already slowed down. However, it stays above the 2.0% target, which means that it is still completely controlled by the Fed. With new tariffs, domestic prices may increase. This may be exacerbated by the impact of the Middle East tension and skyrocketing oil and fuel prices. Their tendency to raise domestic prices may weaken household and consumer budgets. So, their purchasing power and repayment capacity may also decrease. Hence, it must also account for delinquency risks.

Mitigants/Drivers

Initially, an investor may think that OMF is a high-risk investment, and I can't blame him since OMF mainly operates in the consumer finance sector. And if you look at its credit quality, delinquencies are above 1%, which is typical for personal and consumer loans. On a lighter note, delinquencies are still stable on a YoY and QoQ basis despite stubborn inflation. In fact, loans that are past due 90 days decreased to 2.49% from 2.52% YoY.

Concerning inflation and the Middle East tension, I think OMF will still receive some positive spillovers. One is that inflation may attract more borrowers to support consumption and spending. Second, the US Fed may halt the policy easing cycle. This means that its loan yields will not decrease for the meantime because most consumer and credit card loans have variable rates. So, its loans will not be subject to lower repricing rates.

Moreover, OMF is still well-positioned. Again, its personal loans and auto finance loans have fixed rates, which protect them from interest rate volatility. More importantly, most of them are secured or collateralized. For instance 53% of its personal loans are secured by titled property versus only 50% YoY.

Another core strength of the company is its conservative approach to loans. It continues to raise its provisions or allowance for credit losses. In fact, the allowance ratio remains high at 11.54%. This allows OMF to cover delinquencies, which is crucial amid increased macroeconomic volatility. On top of that, it does not exhaust its debt for loans. In fact, only 93% of the total amount is used for lending. That way, it still has excess liquidity in case the interest rate of its borrowings rises. Lastly, it has adequate liquid assets. The combined amount of cash and cash equivalents and investment securities is $2.5B, which can support its overall liquidity. More importantly, investment securities also earn yields, which can raise their interest income even if the Fed raises the interest rate again. So, a weakness in one interest-earning asset can be partially covered by another.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $4.20, so the dividend growth rate is 8.44% based on the five-year CAGR. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium to 7.0% to price in more risks compared to my previous coverage at only 6.5% and the market standard of 5.0%. Lastly, my new beta is now 1.25, which means that OMF has become less volatile. However, this is still higher than 1.00, so it is still more volatile than the S&P 500. Nonetheless, my new TP, or target price, of $88.05 still shows underpricing. But considering the current risks, I added a 20% risk consideration, leading to a lower TP of $70.44. Even if it increases to that level, the stock will remain attractive due to the dividend yield of 6.0%.

Meanwhile, its P/E is still low at only 8.79x. This means that the stock only trades at about 8-9x its EPS. If we use its 2023-2025 compound growth of 11.04%, the PEG will only be 0.80 (8.79/11.04). This means that the stock only trades at about 80% of its actual growth. Ideally, P/E and earnings growth are the same. This can tell us that the stock is still trading at a slightly discounted level.

Technicals

OMF's technicals are still a bit weak after the recent correction. They still warrant some caution if you look at its decreasing 50-day SMA below the 200-day SMA, which reflects the unfavorable market sentiments in February and March. On a lighter note, the price has also started to rebound as buyers reentered after the selloff. It has already crossed above the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is already stronger than the average in the past 50 days. You can also note that the downward trend of the 50-day SMA has somewhat flattened, which suggests that market pessimism has started to wane as the stock becomes reasonably valued.

Meanwhile, its MACD and RSI show that there were two reasons behind the downtrend. First, the stock became overbought. Second, there was a bearish divergence if you look at the increasing price high from December 12 to January 7 versus the decreasing highs of the two indicators on the same dates. This means that selling pressures began and fewer buyers entered after the stock broke above $70. The correction was needed to rebalance the market, especially buying and selling volume. Now, the trend of the price is consistent again with the two indicators. So, the stock uptrend may hold. RSI still warrants some caution as it approaches overbuying again. A dip is possible. But the MACD line is now increasing above the signal line, while the histogram is in positive territory, which means that buying volume outpaces selling volume again.

Conclusion

OneMain Holdings, Inc. is exposed to macroeconomic volatility risks. But its prudent loan management and conservative approach to growth through provisions and collaterals limit the impact of delinquency risks. In addition, its adequate level of liquid assets ensures it can sustain its operations and dividends even if a recession takes place. Valuation is more realistic today with a high dividend yield, while technicals show rebound attempts and renewed bullish signals despite potential overbuying. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Coherent: Don't Bet On This Party Lasting Forever
data.description:
Coherent: Benefiting From The Massive Surge In Opticals

Scale up, scale out, scale across… buzzwords are flying left, right, and center.

Copper, co-packaged optics. Data center interconnects.

At this juncture of the AI boom, I don’t think I need to impress upon you further that the center of gravity has shifted beyond AI chips, right?

I had already discussed in my recent AMD (AMD) writeup, indicating that even the humble CPU is now back in the limelight.

Investors are now asking questions about whether Nvidia’s (NVDA) stranglehold over the ecosystem is finally coming under threat.

As AI shifts into the agentic era, will opportunities in Nvidia still be the only way to play this AI boom?

I’ve always been keen on exploring opportunities beyond Nvidia.

While the stock remains one of my core positions in my portfolio, and also the portfolio as part of my investing group (Ultimate Growth Investing), I also know that we need to continue probing if we want to drive alpha.

In assessing these opportunities in the optical networking spaces, Coherent Corp. (COHR) immediately comes to my mind.

Coherent: Co-Packaged Optics Becoming Front And Center

Not just because its total return of 567% over the past year has stunned me, the company also reportedly possesses one of the most comprehensive portfolios when it comes to optical solutions for the AI infrastructure growth.

Based on the recent OFC event held in March, it seems super clear to me that Coherent’s TAM is expanding rapidly.

It's a key player in the upcoming co-packaged optics growth prospects from the second half of this year, (more significantly through 2027 and beyond).

Thus, it isn’t hard to imagine why investors believe COHR is well-positioned to benefit from just the CPO outlook per se, which “represents a $15bn+ SAM by CY30, as fiber optics join copper interconnects in scale-up architectures."

If you think about the >$10.5 billion in revenue that Coherent is expected to deliver for the fiscal year ending June 2028, I guess it’s obvious that the potential for further gains through the decade is spurring this incredible performance for the stock.

Questions about whether CPO will become a true lever for COHR have received much needed validation in March, when Nvidia (NVDA) decided to invest $2 billion.

That not only strengthens the full tech stack capabilities of COHR's proposition, but it also secures the roadmap for COHR when CPO becomes more critical for Nvidia’s architecture from 2027.

Coherent Has A Full Stack Of Opportunities

Given how comprehensive the company’s portfolio exposure to the AI buildout is, the opportunities aren't limited to CPO.

Even if CPO were to scale later through 2027 for scale-up opportunities, there are four key growth engines that are already in the works, with Optical Circuit Switch, or OCS, already in play right now.

Hence, I believe that helps secure the roadmap to justify its otherwise hefty premium in its valuation after the incredible 1-year performance.

The existing levers relating to the transceivers and other transport and optical components are already helping to keep investors on board.

Wall Street has also gone all in to lift its optimism on COHR’s earnings outlook.

So unless we get a massive structural decline right now hurting margins, I do think that the bullish momentum could carry on for some time to come.

COHR's Margins Worries Aren't Immaterial

However, while COHR investors are enjoying the ride onto cloud nine, I wouldn’t be too quick to throw caution to the wind right now.

It’s no secret that the industry has always been super competitive, as margins really hovered at relatively low levels.

Hence, the volatility in its free cash flow margins could be one possible stumbling block that could keep investors from rerating the stock further, given how expensive its valuation has already baked in the FCF inflection through June 2028.

Plus, when we consider that even among its closer peers, the GAAP operating margins over the past few years don’t quite inspire enough confidence that we should accord such elevated multiples right now.

I’m not saying that Coherent’s TAM expansion and product stack aren’t material to the valuations. Obviously, they are.

And the CPO opportunities through the rest of the decade are no longer theoretical, but are ones where they will become increasingly crucial for scale-up networks.

Because even as copper is still going to play a foundational role in the current paradigm, this shift toward a more hybrid kind of arrangement with CPOs and copper is crucial to overcome the inherent limitation of not being “limited by interconnect reach.”

Obviously that explains why the Coherent management sees such incredible upside noted in its TAM expansion slides, and that also corroborates the ground research relating to the CPO spaces.

COHR Valuations Show Little Room For Disappointment

However, has the market not considered this huge upside potential?

Of course it has, right?

With COHR’s forward earnings now cruising to the 55x level, it’s already well above the averages that used to define the stock in the past five years, somewhere closer to the 21x mark.

So, the market has clearly gone GAGA over the stock, although I must say the print hasn’t quite indicated that a bearish rating is justified.

Meaning, high valuations, but not necessarily pointing to a Sell.

Buy Or Sell COHR Stock?

What I find truly fascinating with the print is that COHR hasn’t exhibited such dramatic weakness that could have caused investors to quickly bail out from their positions to protect their hard-earned profits.

After all, we know from the price action that the stock has had bouts of downside volatility that also caused immense pain in the past.

The periods from 2021/22 and then the sharp pullback toward April 2025 were instances of such downside.

Yet, I must highlight that COHR hasn’t really pulled back since then, which is really remarkable to say the least.

It demonstrates the high conviction bullish momentum built into the setup.

And I guess as long as that TAM gets revised upwards, earnings upgrades should continue to follow.

In such instances, I will still want to look for a possible consolidation zone for a subsequent entry point.

But, it’s one that I’ve not really observed at this point.

The last one that saw dip buyers coming back strongly was at the $52 level, and that was so last year.

For this year and over the past six months, sellers appear to have packed their bags and decided that they will stay out of this to avoid getting overrun by the bulls.

However, COHR’s valuation also informs me that it might get much harder for buyers to push for even higher valuations at this stage.

A possible entry point closer to the $215 level might be worth thinking about, as that was where the recent brief dip in March/April dropped to.

But, at this point, I would encourage you to stay out of this print, unless you are willing to bet that YoY growth rates can continue well beyond the 2030s.

Rating: Hold.

data.title: Coherent Is The Ultimate AI Bottleneck Play
data.description:
Introduction

Coherent Corp. (COHR) is one of the companies seeing major returns amid 2026 market choppiness. I’m very happy to see the stock do so well, as it was one of my Buying suggestions for 2026 at the end of December ’25, as an AI-supply bottleneck, together with Marvell (MRVL), and Fabrinet (FN). As a matter of fact, all three of them have done exceptionally well this year: COHR is up 88% YTD, MRVL +74% YTD, and FN +53% YTD.

I, for myself, have gone with Marvell, because I thought it was suppressed the most in 2025, but I’m glad to see all three did way better than the market. Amid the fears surrounding large CapEx spending, the market could have dragged the main suppliers of the data-center buildout lower, but gladly, that didn’t happen. Even though investors are worried that this capacity is needless, it doesn’t cancel the fact that the suppliers majorly benefit.

Another reason I thought it would be nice to take a closer look at Coherent is that just yesterday, I broke down POET Technologies (POET), which is possibly the next age of transceivers and data center networking, as it is working towards giving up on electricity for the data-center buildout entirely, using only light. It is still far from achieving this goal, but this is a direct attempt to disrupt companies like Coherent. At the same time, Coherent is a large industry leader, unbothered by the smaller peers, which has some mind-blowing developments on its own, and some large partnerships, which support its leading position.

Also, the company has quarterly earnings in about two weeks from now, on May 5th, after the market, and it looks like Wall Street expects to see a major beat, with 15 positive vs. 1 negative EPS revisions, and 16 positive vs 0 negative revenue revisions. This could be both a blessing and a curse, since usually, when the sentiment before the earnings is this positive, the stock begins gaining weeks before the report. But if the company misses even slightly, this positive sentiment can turn into a major negative catalyst, since the market expectations are through the roof. In this article, I’ll take a look at the latest quarter and try to understand what to expect this time around.

Coherent’s Competitive Position

Quick disclaimer - I don’t have a technical background, but I usually try to understand and explain the technology behind the stock. I believe my understanding is sufficient for investing purposes, but some oversimplification is probable, as I’m not trying to break down the technology itself, just what drives the demand, and as a result, the stock price.

The reason Coherent and its peers are on the rise is that, due to the rapid AI adoption, not only is the compute or memory becoming a bottleneck, but also networking. Before, electricity was moved using copper wires, and it was good enough, since distances between the chips and the demand for bandwidth weren’t large. But now, not only are copper wires insufficient, but also electricity isn’t fast enough to allow modern data centers to work as they were planned to work. For this reason, companies like Coherent are doing so well, since they are developing and producing transceiver modules, which are devices able to convert an electrical signal into a light signal and vice versa, allowing it to be sent via fiber-optic cables, which provide a faster solution and higher bandwidth.

But there are different optical modules, depending on the request of the technology it powers, and this is where the innovation of Coherent comes in. As modern data centers are being built, not only are the newest, most powerful GPUs being used for that, but also, those GPUs are installed into racks, effectively creating small supercomputers, which, when connected, make a very large and very powerful supercomputer.

The difficulty is that, before those “supercomputers” were created, data centers had way less demand for data transmitted per second. Today, for large hyperscalers with enormous cloud data centers, 800G (gigabits per second of data transmission) has become a new standard in the last few years, but soon, even that won’t be enough if many racks are connected. Just to understand how much lower the benchmark was, in the past, up to 100G optical modules were enough for a regular data center, and for a large enterprise, 10G or 25G would be sufficient.

Just a decade ago, 100G was the biggest breakthrough in technology, but today, not only are the companies widely adopting 800G, 1.6T (1600G) is on the way, looking forward to 3.2T and 6.4T.

At the same time, even though everyone is trying so hard to avoid copper usage, and companies that provide those solutions are on a roll, Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said that:

Nvidia would use copper and co-packaged optics in both its upcoming Vera Rubin Ultra and Feynman lines, with both options advancing in parallel.

After the news, Coherent and some of the optics peers fell, although I don’t view this necessarily as a bad thing. They have a massive partnership, and Nvidia has invested $2 billion in Coherent, which means that the technology is definitely in demand. The second way of looking at this: the fact that optics is being used only partially means that the company still has growth opportunities not only together with the overall GPU and data center market, but also as a substitute to other solutions like copper. That opportunity is still there.

Q2 2026

Talking about the last quarter, the company saw a rapid revenue growth of 22% YoY to $1.69 billion, with an expanding gross margin, and a 35% growth in Earnings Per Share. The Datacenter & Communication segment takes up 72% of the overall revenue, being the main driver of the growth, increasing by an impressive 34%.

The management not only says that this is the main and fastest-growing segment, but they expect the demand there to continue throughout the whole year, powering companies' rapid growth both in Q3 and Q4 of 2026, accelerating even more in fiscal 2027:

In particular, we expect continued strong sequential revenue growth in both our March and June quarters, and we expect our fiscal '27 revenue growth rate to exceed our fiscal '26 growth rate.

To be more specific, 800G and 1.6T transceivers are what drive the demand, together with some additional technologies that allow faster bandwidth, like OCS (Optical Circuit Switching), and CPO solutions (co-packaged optics). This, by the way, is what Jensen Huang referred to when he said they’ll be using it together with copper:

The key growth drivers that we see over the coming quarters are growth in both 800 gig and 1.6T transceivers, growth from the ramps of new products such as OCS and CPO solutions and ongoing exceptionally strong demand in our products for DCI and scale across.

The demand is in fact so strong that the company has a book-to-bill ratio of 4x, meaning they are simply unable to ship as much, as companies are ordering from them. For this reason, the peers of Coherent, like Marvell and Lumentum (LITE), are also seeing the same growth. Even though the industry is competitive, the demand is still bigger than the market-wide supply.

In Q2, we experienced another step function increase in our data center bookings with a book-to-bill ratio that exceeded 4x as customer demand continues to increase and customers place orders further out in time, which provides us with strong visibility for the coming quarters.

As a result, Coherent spends a lot of money to ramp up production with additional manufacturing capacity in the hope of capturing as many orders as possible. The company wants to double its indium phosphide production by Q4, which would allow it to ramp up 6-inch wafer production. A 6-inch wafer allows producing 4 times more chips than a 3-inch wafer, and it will also be twice as cheap.

We expect 1.6T to ramp significantly over the coming quarters with the early phase of the ramp driven by our EML and Silicon Photonics-based transceivers followed by our 200-gig VCSEL-based 1.6T transceivers ramping in the second half of this calendar year. On the supply side to address the extraordinary growth in demand, we are investing in the rapid expansion of our production capacity. For example, we significantly increased our indium phosphide production capacity in Q2, and we are executing on track to our plan to double our internal indium phosphide production capacity by the fourth quarter of this calendar year.

I also found it quite interesting that even with growing demand, the company is working on becoming more effective and productive, boosting operational excellence. They are doing so by closing the site, which didn’t perform well, and they are turning their focus to what is profitable and in demand today. Even though the same as with all of the semiconductor industry, the business of this company is cyclical. As long as the cycle continues, this is a correct strategy, in my opinion:

Last week, we completed the sale of our product division based in Munich, Germany that makes tools for materials processing. The sale of this product division is expected to be immediately accretive to both gross margin and EPS. As a result of this sale and other operational streamlining initiatives, we exited 10 sites over the past quarter which brings the total number of sites that we've either sold or exited to 33 over the past roughly 6 quarters since we began this initiative. We plan to continue to streamline our footprint and exit additional underutilized and unnecessary sites over the coming quarters.

Guidance for the next quarter is optimistic, with $1.77 billion in revenue expected in the middle of the given range, which would mean almost 5% increase QoQ, and 18% growth YoY. Even the lowest range of the revenue guidance would mean 13% revenue growth, which would be a solid increase, since Q3 2025 was an exceptionally strong quarter, showing 24% ToY growth. I remind you, the outlook for the fiscal 2027 is even more optimistic, and the management expects a growth ratio higher than that of 2026.

Gross margin is expected to be between 38.5% to 40.5%, which will be ahead of that a year ago, and if higher than 39%, ahead of the last quarter. Earnings per share are expected to be in line with the Q2 on the lowest end of the range, and to be 15% higher than a quarter before on the high end of the range. On the high end of the range, the growth YoY will be over 62%. The company isn’t guiding for GAAP results, as they can’t predict what the impact of some of the charges will be. Among those are the Munich site, consolidation, currency swings, etc.

By the way, EPS is growing way faster than the revenue and the margins for all of the fixed costs the company has already paid. Basically, the growth they are bringing in right now is kind of cheaper and cheaper, since it comes from R&D and fabs that are ready, and their SG&A or marketing costs are not growing as fast, because the demand is organic.

Valuation

Of course, the main pain is the valuation. On all of the regular metrics, the stock looks incredibly expensive, but that premium looks way healthier on the PEG ratio. PEG ratio is the best way to look at the stocks in the fast-growing industries, since it normalizes the sector for growth, and we can see the real comparison with the peers.

According to the PEG ratio, the stock trades at 1.7 times, ahead of 1.46 for the sector, but it is a premium of about 16%. It is not cheap in any way, but it is also not sky-high as the traditional P/E suggests.

Conclusion

Coherent is a great company in a fast-growing industry. The management makes sure they stay operationally excellent and does everything to capture the fast-growing demand for their products. At the same time, after the rally, COHR stock trades at a high valuation multiple, not only leaving no room for mistakes, but also suggesting that some cooling-off is possible after the rally.

When buying Marvell myself, the stock traded at a premium on the traditional price-to-earnings metrics, but looked cheap on the PEG ratio. This time around, I’m afraid most of the good news has already been priced in, and I would wait for some correction to start a position in COHR stock myself.

data.title: Axcelis Technologies: Medium-Term May Be Priced In, But Overall, A Lot Of Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Axcelis Technologies, Inc. (ACLS) shot up since my last article back in January, and now that we have the full year’s performance released, I want to revisit the past year and see what is still ahead for the company in 2026 and beyond. The share price seems to reflect a positive outlook going forward because the past year does not justify such a price increase in one year.

There’s no need to sell your shares right now, and I suggest holding for a couple of more years at least, since I believe there will be a lot of gains to come.

By the Numbers

Over the last year, the company’s revenues continued their downtrend. In the latest financial year report, revenues came in 17.5% lower than last year. The company continues to be in a cyclical digestion phase across its biggest end markets, with power and general mature customers working through prior capacity additions, while the Chinese demand was digesting earlier investments. It’s not a great look, but this was an expected outcome that was communicated many times throughout the year.

It does seem that there will be some positives in the future, as the graph suggests that there may be a bottom set.

In the latest yearly report, we can also see how the company’s profitability came in, and it is not great either. Gross margins improved slightly to 44.9%, up 20bps, and operating margins took a dive to 14.2% from 20.7%. Operating expenses increased while revenues continued to decline. Unfortunately for ACLS, when system sales softened, the cost structure didn’t move down with volume, so the fixed-cost base weighed on its operating leverage. The only way to improve this is by increasing utilization rates so the fixed costs can be spread out much more efficiently.

The company’s balance sheet remains quite formidable, with over $145m in cash and equivalents, as well as $229m in ST investments against zero debt, apart from operating lease liabilities. This is a great place to be when its operations are soft and are on their way to turning around. The company can focus all of its capital on maintaining its competitiveness and weather the downturn with no issues. It’s hard to go out of business when there is no one knocking on your door and asking for the money that they lent you.

The company’s cash flow did come down slightly from last year, but it is still over $118m, and with capex spending of around $11.3m, its free cash flow was around $107m, which is still pretty good considering the downturn in operations.

Overall, 2025 has not been particularly impressive for Axcelis. The demand for the products has been soft, which squeezed margins considerably compared to last year due to lower system volume in power, general mature, and China, which still accounts for around 32% of total revenues. This type of performance doesn’t warrant its share price going up over 180% in just one year, which means that the analysts and investors are focusing on what is still ahead for the company.

Comments on the Outlook

Upcoming Quarter

For Q1 ’26, analysts are expecting the company to do $0.71 and $0.44 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $195m in revenues. This translates into around 1% growth compared to last year, which may signal that the end of declining revenues is upon us. Management also guided $195m in revenues and non-GAAP EPS of $0.71, but around 38 cents in GAAP diluted EPS. For the full year, the company is expecting to see flat revenue performance, which I see as a positive because it does point to the company approaching the bottom. The growth in memory is expected to offset the continued weakness in power and mature.

Memory Revival

Historically, memory has been a poor segment compared to its power/SiC offerings. We don’t get a precise breakdown of revenues in products, so we don’t know precisely what the memory revenue is as % of total revenues these days, but it is likely around 15%-25%. This is a big enough number that will most likely move the revenue needle considerably now that it is looking to come back to life. Memory players like Micron (MU), Western Digital (WDC), and SanDisk (SNDK) have been one of the best performers in terms of share price gains over the last year, and all because of capacity constraints, which allows them to massively increase the prices of memory products directly, leading to an increase in profitability. We are currently in a super cycle of memory, and anyone exposed to AI and memory like DRAM and HBM has seen their share price performance beat the broader index market by a massive margin. So, of course, Axcelis is finally going to see some traction in its memory business. With Micron being a direct beneficiary of the AI hype as it sells HBM and DRAM into the AI stack, Axcelis benefits when memory manufacturers convert price strength and demand visibility into fab equipment orders. In the past, this conversion lagged the pricing cycle by around 6-18 months, so now, ACLS is seeing some of the conversion flowing through in 2026, and that is only the beginning.

The management said that they expect large tool orders to come more meaningfully when new fabs open at the end of 2026 and into 2027, but the company is already seeing that customers are trying to push through a few more tool orders now, instead of waiting.

I think for this reason, we are seeing a similar situation in the share price performance as we saw in the pure AI memory plays of MU, SNDK, and WDC. Capacity constraints are the key drivers of share price over the last year because investors are already pricing in much higher revenue and profitability that will eventually come sometime in the back end of 2026 and throughout 2027. I am also very bullish on the super cycle lasting much longer than 2027 and well into 2028. The insatiable hunger for power that we see from the hyperscalers right now is not going to end very soon, since we know that the top 5 or 6 hyperscalers this year will spend around $750B in AI capex.

For next year, I am assuming the same spending pattern to come through, which could mean the top hyperscalers will spend $1T in capex on AI. I think the worst case is actually them slowing down, but I don’t think it is going to be a meaningful slowdown. Maybe down to $500B, which is still a ridiculous amount of capital to be tossed around by just a handful of companies.

Veeco Merger

I covered the Veeco (VECO) merger in my previous article, so I won’t go into that right now; however, I will say that when it does happen, the company will see a massive TAM expansion. With Axcelis doing ion implantation while VECO doing laser annealing, the merged company will offer both steps as an integrated solution, which will create yield guarantees that the standalone tools cannot simply match. The two functions are consecutive in nature on the fab floor, so by integrating them, they will offer a meaningful value proposition for chipmakers running high-precision power or AI chip processes where yield losses can cost quite dearly. The merger is viewed quite positively overall and will transform the company tremendously.

Both sides of shareholders approved the deal in early February, and many regulatory clearances were already passed as well. One regulator remains, and that is China’s SAMR. In the latest conference call, the phrasing around China was very deliberate. They said that they are “actively engaging with the authorities in China,” but did not suggest there was an imminent approval on the cards. They still expect the deal to close in 2026, but according to reports, SAMR is taking unusually long to approve the deal. According to Ion Analytics, SAMR usually takes around 18.2 days to review the deal. Still, this time around, it is taking a more cautious approach when handling reviews that involve U.S. companies.

So, it all depends on China. Is it going to pass? That is the hardest question to answer. I don’t think the deal creates a monopoly since the two companies are not in the same area of functions. Also, neither company is the biggest player in the relevant markets, so I don’t think the narrative will be monopoly concerns. A block of a merger by SAMR is a rare occurrence in general, so I am sure it will go through eventually.

Closing Comments

If you already own Axcelis, I don’t see a reason to sell; however, I am not looking to add AI play to my portfolio when I am actively looking to diversify from that specific risk. I am especially not looking to add a company that is trading at a forward P/E ratio of 36x. It seems that the near-term boost has already been priced in, especially after B. Riley Financial upgraded it to a Buy recently and gave a price target of $150.
