There is no reason to sell such a winner right now, especially if you are sitting on a decent profit. Let it ride for the next couple of years, until we know what is going to happen to the overall AI demand in late 2027, because for now, I see further growth ahead.

data.title: 2 'Set And Forget' Dividend Legends To Buy Now
data.description:
One of the reasons why I have decided to follow an income-based strategy is that my portfolio results (and progress) are not impacted by what others think.

The success of price-focused strategies is determined by the function of earnings growth and valuation multiple. None of these can be predicted with a robust precision, especially the latter.

There are just so many variables that dictate the path of valuation multiples: e.g., overall sentiment in the market, interest rates, inflation, geopolitics, which President wins and so on.

As a result of this, it may happen that in one year I can exceed my annual return target, the next year have even better success, and then in the third year suddenly fall back to square one. And the worst what could happen is that this "Year 3" coincides with the moment when I have to transition to a withdrawal phase.

Having this kind of unpredictability embedded in my portfolio which is something that will have a significant bearing on a big part of my life is simply unacceptable for me.

I know what the financial theory says (the Miller-Modigliani dividend irrelevance theory): dividends don't create value but rather the underlying cash flows and earrings growth. But as a human being, I have my biases.

I simply must have predictability, stability and all working out as I have planned out to keep peace of mind. And if it means paying some price for that like not capturing alpha-like returns, so be it.

To achieve this, there are two simple things required:

  • Properly calibrated expectation: investment progress and the final goal have to be linked to current income streams (price performance has to be excluded from that equation). For example, the goal could be to build up a portfolio which produces $100,000 of dividends a year and for that to happen until a certain date each year the portfolio has to generate $7,000 higher dividends than in the previous year.
  • Durable investments: the majority of the holdings (or portfolio cash flows) must be associated with investments that can sustain their dividend payments across different market cycles, for multiple decades in the future. Otherwise the predictability (sleep-well-at-night) aspect is not there, just as for price-based strategies.

Now, with this in mind, let me share two durable dividend producing picks for "zero nighttime anxiety" and maximized investment result (progress) predictability.

#1: MAIN, 7.9%

Main Street Capital (MAIN) is a BDC, which provides private credit financing to lower-middle-market (or LMM) companies. The optics already here do not look good because of the word "private credit".

However, even though I am a structural BDC bull and prefer several other BDCs over MAIN, when it comes to maximized income stability and stress-free cash flows, I view MAIN as the best BDC pick which nicely fulfills these two aspects.

MAIN has one of the longest track-records in the BDC sector (BIZD); almost 20 years as a publicly traded company. And this is how it has performed since its IPO:

It is an income investor's dream scenario: very gradual price appreciation with juicy dividends which overtime compound to something big and beautiful. The only thing that is required is a) being patient and b) reinvesting the collected dividends to accelerate compounding.

Now, the question is whether future will be materially different from the past.

There are two components to this answer:

1) Price direction: as an income-investors I really don't care so much about where the price will go from here as long as the underlying fundamentals and dividends remain stable.

2) Dividend stability and growth: this is what is critically important for me. The good thing is that if we look at the fundamentals, we will see all the major data points signaling that MAIN should be able to deliver consistent dividends going forward.

For the dividends to stay safe, there are a couple of pre-conditions that must be met. I will underscore the three main ones.

First, MAIN has the fourth least leveraged balance sheet. The sector average debt-to-equity is 1.25x, but for MAIN the metric stands at 0.81x. This kind of level helps keep the consequences from potential hiccups in the portfolio muted as there is no meaningful leverage that could magnify the downside. Also, MAIN's balance sheet is rated at investment grade, which confirms the inherent resiliency.

Second, MAIN has a top-quality portfolio. It is not just the below sector average non-accrual statistic which validates that, but also the financial situation at the portfolio company level. For instance, the average interest coverage ratio for MAIN's portfolio companies is 4.1x, which is the second strongest metric in the entire BDC space (and I'm talking about both private and public BDCs here).

Third, MAIN has several levers to pull in order to drive earning growth, which is, obviously, positive for the dividend (the best defense is a good offense).

The leverage aspect that I mentioned above is an important one as it shows how much wiggle room MAIN has in using debt to expand portfolio while not assuming elevated financial risks. Another aspect, which comes in handy in the context of earnings growth (and dividend sustainability) is the sector-leading base dividend coverage, which stands at 142%. From the outearned base dividend MAIN can distribute enticing special dividends (which I'm not factoring into my long-term portfolio cash flow projections as they are inherently volatile) or use this capital to grow the portfolio.

Finally, the fact that MAIN trades at the highest P/NAV in the sector is a blessing and a curse.

The curse is that it, theoretically, creates a more difficult environment for MAIN to register strong price appreciation that would be, say, driven by multiple expansion. Again, as a pure income investor I don't care about this aspect.

The blessing is that MAIN can source fresh equity via accretive share issuances. It is just yet another source of capital for MAIN to grow the portfolio and drive NII per share growth.

Plus, in the past 12 months, only two BDCs increased their base dividends (by 4.1% on a year-over-year basis). MAIN was one of them, which serves as the icing on the cake and confirms its 'sleep-well-at-night' appeal.

#2: EPD, 6.0%

Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) is one of the biggest midstream partnerships or MLPs out there with a market cap of over $79 billion.

Just as MAIN, EPD has perfectly clean dividend track-record, meaning that there have not been any base distributions cuts since the IPO. In fact, EPD is a dividend aristocrat with 28 consecutive years of distribution increases.

If we look at the performance chart of EPD below and compare it to that of MAIN above, we would notice a great similarity:

A steady price dynamic with strongly compounding current income component.

Now, just as for MAIN, the question is whether EPD has what it takes to "walk my talk" (i.e., to deliver durable cash flows without recording NAV or value impairments).

In my humble opinion, EPD is more than well-positioned to deliver on that.

I will give you 3 reasons.

First, EPD has, arguably, the best balance sheet in the entire midstream (MLP and c-corp) space. It has an upper investment grade balance sheet, which is quite rare. Most midstream businesses either don't have IG ratings at all or have them at medium IG level. Another aspect, which makes the balance sheet (or rather financial risk angle) even more compelling is the fact that 98% of borrowings are assumed at fixed rate. The weighted average cost of debt is 4.7%, which is close to the current market level financing rates.

Second, and this is just as important as the first point, the EPD's underlying business model is inherently de-risked thanks to the presence of very favorable contracts. Namely, the lion's share of EPD's EBITDA generation stems from long-term "take-or-pay" contracts, which are stipulated with large and bankable corporates (i.e., infrastructure users). These contracts come with minimal volume and price clauses, which mitigate both volumetric and commodity price risks. On top of that, most of these contracts are linked to annual fee escalators or a CPI factor, which add a nice organic growth flavor to the already enticing situation. In essence, these points renders EPD similar to a fixed income play (especially if we couple it with the first point above).

Third, EPD carries a huge margin of safety for its distributions. The current distributions are more than covered by the distributable cash flow generation; the ratio is 1.7x. As in MAIN's case, EPD has the luxury to use these surplus (retained) cash flows to fund growth. Special distributions have not been EPD's thing (not a single special since 1998). Given that the balance sheet is already at an upper IG level, debt reduction activity is very unlikely. What EPD has communicated is that it will consider utilizing the confirmed buyback capacity, while ensuring steady distribution growth and, importantly, a healthy organic CapEx program. Namely, EPD is in a position to not only strongly preserve the current distributions, but also grow them on a go forward basis.

Finally, what makes me extra comfortable with EPD is that 33% of common units are owned by the management and affiliates. It just shows how strong an alignment there is between the insiders and us, long-term investors. It is the same thing for MAIN as it is an internally managed BDC (as opposed to "90%+" of other peers which are externally managed).
